subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Types of Domestic Contracts — Marriage Contract, Cohabitation Agreement, Separation Agreement
fact_pattern: Two individuals, Sam and Alex, are planning to marry. Before the wedding, Sam wants to enter into an agreement with Alex that specifies how their property will be divided in the event of marriage breakdown and limits any future spousal support claims. Their lawyer advises them on the appropriate type of domestic contract.
call_of_question: Under the Family Law Act, what is the appropriate domestic contract for parties who are about to marry and wish to govern their property and support rights in the event of marriage breakdown?

options.A: Cohabitation agreement — because they have not yet married.
options.B: Separation agreement — because this is the agreement that governs marriage breakdown.
options.C: Marriage contract — under FLA s.52, two persons who are married or who intend to marry may enter into a marriage contract to govern ownership of or division of property, support obligations, and other matters in the event of separation, death, or other circumstances.
options.D: There is no recognized domestic contract for parties intending to marry; Sam and Alex must wait until after the wedding to negotiate their rights.

explanation: Family Law Act s.52(1) defines a marriage contract as an agreement entered into by two persons who are married to each other or intend to marry, in which they agree on their respective rights and obligations under the marriage or on separation, annulment, dissolution of the marriage or death. A marriage contract (also called a prenuptial agreement) may address property division, spousal support, and other matters. It is the appropriate vehicle before marriage. A cohabitation agreement governs unmarried cohabiting couples; a separation agreement governs the terms of an existing separation.
why_A_wrong: A cohabitation agreement under FLA s.53 is designed for persons who are cohabiting or intend to cohabit outside of marriage. Since Sam and Alex intend to marry, a marriage contract — not a cohabitation agreement — is the appropriate instrument.
why_B_wrong: A separation agreement under FLA s.54 is entered into by persons who are married or who cohabited and are living separate and apart. Sam and Alex have not yet separated; a separation agreement is not available before marriage breakdown.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. FLA s.52 authorizes a marriage contract (prenuptial agreement) for persons who are married or intend to marry, governing their rights on separation, dissolution, or death.
why_D_wrong: FLA s.52 expressly recognizes marriage contracts entered into by persons who intend to marry (prenuptial agreements). It is not necessary to wait until after the wedding.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Types of Domestic Contracts — FLA ss.52-54
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 52(1) (marriage contract), s. 53 (cohabitation agreement), s. 54 (separation agreement)

subject: family_law
fact_pattern: Rachel and her husband Tom negotiate the terms of a separation agreement during mediation. After reaching agreement, they shake hands and verbally confirm the deal but do not sign anything. Rachel later argues the verbal agreement is a binding separation agreement. Tom disputes this.
call_of_question: Under the Family Law Act, what are the formal requirements for a valid domestic contract, and does Rachel and Tom's verbal agreement satisfy them?

options.A: A verbal agreement confirmed in good faith is a valid and enforceable domestic contract under the Family Law Act.
options.B: Under FLA s.55(1), a domestic contract is unenforceable unless it is made in writing, signed by both parties, and witnessed; a verbal agreement does not satisfy these requirements and is not a binding domestic contract.
options.C: A domestic contract is valid if two witnesses observe the verbal agreement, even without a written document.
options.D: A domestic contract requires notarization by a notary public to be valid; a signature alone is insufficient.

explanation: Family Law Act s.55(1) provides that a domestic contract and an amendment to a domestic contract are unenforceable unless made in writing, signed by the parties, and witnessed. All three requirements — writing, signature, and witness — must be met for the contract to be valid and enforceable. Rachel and Tom's verbal handshake agreement does not satisfy any of these requirements. Without a written and witnessed signed document, the purported agreement is unenforceable as a domestic contract under the FLA.
why_A_wrong: Verbal agreements are specifically excluded from the definition of enforceable domestic contracts under FLA s.55(1). A handshake or oral confirmation does not create a binding domestic contract regardless of the parties' good faith.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. FLA s.55(1) requires the domestic contract to be in writing, signed by both parties, and witnessed. A verbal agreement does not meet any of these requirements.
why_C_wrong: Two witnesses observing a verbal agreement does not satisfy FLA s.55(1). The requirements are conjunctive: the contract must be in writing AND signed AND witnessed. Oral agreements, however witnessed, are not enforceable domestic contracts.
why_D_wrong: Notarization is not a requirement for a valid domestic contract under the FLA. The three requirements under s.55(1) are: writing, signature by both parties, and a witness to each signature. Notarization adds evidentiary weight but is not a legal requirement.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Formal Validity — FLA s.55
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 55(1)

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Grounds for Setting Aside — FLA s.56(4)
fact_pattern: Katerina signed a separation agreement one week before filing for divorce. She alleges that her husband Leon handed her the agreement at 11 pm, told her to sign it immediately or he would destroy her relationship with their children, and refused to allow her time to see a lawyer. She signed under those circumstances. She now seeks to have the agreement set aside.
call_of_question: Which ground under FLA s.56(4) is most directly applicable to Katerina's circumstances?

options.A: Unconscionability — the terms of the agreement are grossly unfair.
options.B: Failure to disclose significant assets — Leon hid assets from Katerina before signing.
options.C: Duress or undue influence — Katerina's signing was coerced by Leon's threats regarding the children, made under time pressure and without access to independent legal advice; this engages both duress (coercion by threat) and potentially undue influence.
options.D: Fraud — Leon deceived Katerina about the legal effect of the contract.

explanation: Family Law Act s.56(4) provides that a court may set aside a domestic contract or provision if a party failed to disclose significant assets/debts, did not understand the nature/consequences of the contract, or 'otherwise in accordance with the law of contract.' The grounds 'in accordance with the law of contract' import common law and equity grounds including duress and undue influence. Leon's threat to destroy Katerina's relationship with the children (a threat designed to coerce her into signing) accompanied by denial of time to obtain legal advice constitutes duress (or at minimum, undue influence). These are the most directly applicable grounds on these specific facts.
why_A_wrong: Unconscionability is a ground for setting aside under FLA s.56(4), but it requires showing that the contract was so grossly unfair as to shock the conscience of the court. The facts do not describe the terms of the agreement as grossly unfair; the concern here is the coercive process of signing, which is better captured by duress.
why_B_wrong: Failure to disclose significant assets under FLA s.56(4)(a) requires evidence that Leon concealed assets or liabilities. The facts do not describe non-disclosure of assets; the issue is coercive conduct at the time of signing.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Leon's threats and time pressure with denial of legal advice constitute duress or undue influence under FLA s.56(4), specifically through the 'in accordance with the law of contract' gateway importing these equitable and common law doctrines.
why_D_wrong: Fraud requires a misrepresentation of an existing fact. The facts do not describe Leon deceiving Katerina about the content or legal effect of the agreement; they describe coercive pressure. Duress and undue influence better capture the conduct described.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Setting Aside Domestic Contracts — FLA s.56(4)
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 56(4)

tested_concepts: FLA s.56(4) grounds for setting aside

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Non-Disclosure of Significant Assets — FLA s.56(4)(a)
fact_pattern: Before signing a marriage contract, Gerald disclosed his home (value $500,000) but did not disclose his private investment portfolio worth $1.2 million or his RRSP holdings of $400,000. His spouse Linda did not know these assets existed. The marriage later broke down and Linda seeks to set aside the marriage contract.
call_of_question: What is the effect of Gerald's non-disclosure under FLA s.56(4)(a)?

options.A: Non-disclosure of assets is not a ground for setting aside a domestic contract; only duress or fraud can have that effect.
options.B: Linda must prove that she would not have signed the contract had she known of the assets; without that proof, non-disclosure has no effect.
options.C: Under FLA s.56(4)(a), a court may set aside a domestic contract where a party failed to disclose significant assets or debts existing at the time of the contract; Gerald's non-disclosure of the $1.2 million investment portfolio and $400,000 RRSP is likely sufficient to justify setting aside the marriage contract.
options.D: Gerald's non-disclosure automatically voids the marriage contract without the need for a court application.

explanation: Family Law Act s.56(4)(a) provides that a court may set aside a domestic contract or a provision where a party failed to disclose to the other significant assets or significant debts or other liabilities existing when the domestic contract was made. Non-disclosure of a $1.2 million investment portfolio and a $400,000 RRSP — while disclosing only a $500,000 home — constitutes a significant omission of material assets that existed at the time of signing. Linda has strong grounds to seek judicial rescission of the marriage contract. The court has discretion and will consider all circumstances including what the parties would likely have agreed had full disclosure been made.
why_A_wrong: Non-disclosure of significant assets is expressly listed as a statutory ground for setting aside a domestic contract under FLA s.56(4)(a). It is one of three enumerated grounds alongside lack of understanding and other contract law grounds.
why_B_wrong: FLA s.56(4)(a) does not require Linda to prove that she would have refused to sign had she known of all the assets. The court has discretion to set aside the contract upon proof of significant non-disclosure, considering all the circumstances.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. FLA s.56(4)(a) is directly engaged by Gerald's failure to disclose approximately $1.6 million in assets at the time of the marriage contract, providing Linda with a strong basis to seek to set aside the contract.
why_D_wrong: Non-disclosure does not automatically void the contract. The court must exercise its discretion under s.56(4)(a) after a formal application. The contract remains in effect unless and until a court sets it aside.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Setting Aside — Non-Disclosure FLA s.56(4)(a)
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 56(4)(a)

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Limits on Domestic Contracts — Child Support and Access Under FLA s.56(1)
fact_pattern: In their separation agreement, Ian and Donna include a clause specifying that no child support will ever be payable by either party, and that Ian waives his right to seek access to the children. Two years later, Ian seeks access to the children and Donna applies for child support. Ian argues the waiver in the separation agreement bars his access claim and Donna's support claim.
call_of_question: To what extent are clauses in a domestic contract governing child support and parenting (access/contact) binding on the parties and the court?

options.A: Domestic contract clauses regarding child support and access are fully binding and can never be overridden by the court.
options.B: Under FLA s.56(1), a domestic contract provision respecting the education, moral training, or custody of or access to children may be disregarded by the court where the best interests of the child require it; similarly, child support provisions in domestic contracts may be overridden where the contracted amount is not in accordance with the applicable child support guidelines.
options.C: Domestic contracts cannot address child support or access at all; these issues must always be determined exclusively by a court.
options.D: A child support waiver in a separation agreement is valid and binding; the parties may contract out of their right to seek child support forever.

explanation: Family Law Act s.56(1) provides that in the determination of a matter respecting the education, moral training, custody of or access to a child, the court is not bound by a provision of a domestic contract respecting such matters and shall disregard it if, in the opinion of the court, to do so is in the best interests of the child. As for child support, no domestic contract can permanently waive or extinguish child support; courts will override contractual provisions that are inconsistent with the applicable child support guidelines (Miglin v. Miglin, [2003] 1 SCR 303, applied to child support principle). Ian's waiver of access is not binding on him if he later seeks access, and Donna's contractual waiver of child support is not binding on the child's best interests.
why_A_wrong: Domestic contract provisions on child support and access are explicitly not binding on the court under FLA s.56(1). The best interests of the child always override private contractual arrangements in this domain.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. FLA s.56(1) allows courts to disregard domestic contract provisions on education, moral training, custody, and access in the child's best interests; child support cannot be permanently waived by contract.
why_C_wrong: Domestic contracts may and routinely do address child support and access arrangements. FLA s.56(1) does not prohibit this; it simply ensures these provisions can be overridden by the court in the child's best interests.
why_D_wrong: Child support belongs to the child, not the parent. A parent cannot permanently waive a child's right to support through a domestic contract. Any provision purporting to do so is unenforceable and subject to override by the court.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Limits on Domestic Contracts — Child Support and Access
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 56(1)

tested_concepts: FLA s.56(1) court not bound by child provisions

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Marriage Contract vs Separation Agreement — Key Differences
fact_pattern: Eleanor and Frank are negotiating their post-separation arrangements. They are currently married but have been separated for six months. During the marriage, they executed a marriage contract that addressed property division. Now separated, Eleanor's lawyer drafts a document labelled 'Marriage Contract — Separation Arrangements' to settle spousal support and the matrimonial home. Frank's lawyer raises a concern about the label. Eleanor argues the label does not matter legally because both types of contracts are domestic contracts under Part IV of the FLA.
call_of_question: What is the legal consequence, if any, of using a marriage contract rather than a separation agreement to settle the terms of an existing separation?

options.A: There is no legal consequence; both instruments are domestic contracts under FLA Part IV and are fully interchangeable once properly executed.
options.B: A marriage contract is invalid if executed after separation; only a separation agreement has legal force once the parties are living separate and apart.
options.C: Under the FLA, a separation agreement under s.54 is the legally correct instrument for parties who are already living separate and apart; a marriage contract under s.52 governs rights during or arising from the subsisting marriage, not post-separation settlement; using the wrong type of contract may create ambiguity about enforceability and may not qualify for the specific statutory protections and limitations applicable to separation agreements under FLA ss.55–56.
options.D: A marriage contract may validly address all post-separation matters including spousal support and property; the label is legally irrelevant because the FLA treats all Part IV domestic contracts identically for enforcement purposes.

explanation: Family Law Act s.52 defines a marriage contract as an agreement between persons who are married or intend to marry, dealing with rights and obligations during the marriage or on separation, annulment, dissolution, or death. FLA s.54 defines a separation agreement as an agreement entered into by persons who have cohabited and are now living separate and apart, in which they agree on their respective rights and obligations. Although both are Part IV domestic contracts, they are distinct instruments designed for different phases of the relationship. A marriage contract executed post-separation may create ambiguity about whether it properly falls within s.52 — which governs marriage-phase planning — versus the s.54 separation agreement, which triggers specific statutory provisions including FLA s.56(1) (court may override parenting provisions) and s.56(4) (grounds for setting aside). Mislabelling may affect enforcement, create interpretive disputes, and may not engage the intended statutory regime. Best practice is to use a separation agreement (s.54) for post-separation settlements.
why_A_wrong: Exception Trap. While marriage contracts and separation agreements are both Part IV domestic contracts, they are not fully interchangeable. Each is governed by distinct definitional provisions (ss.52 vs 54) and may engage different enforceability considerations. The label and proper statutory characterization are legally significant.
why_B_wrong: Overstatement Trap. A marriage contract is not automatically invalid if executed after separation. FLA s.52 is broad enough to include agreements between married persons addressing rights on separation. However, the parties are better served by using the correct instrument — a s.54 separation agreement — to engage the full statutory framework applicable to post-separation settlements.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. FLA s.54 is the appropriate instrument for settling post-separation affairs; using a marriage contract label creates ambiguity about the applicable statutory regime and may affect enforceability under ss.55–56.
why_D_wrong: Pragmatic Bluff. The two types of contracts are not legally identical for enforcement. The specific provisions of s.56 (setting aside, best interests override, equalization exclusion) apply in the context of the instrument type. Treating them as identical ignores legally significant distinctions in the FLA's Part IV framework.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Marriage Contract vs Separation Agreement — FLA ss.52 and 54
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 52 (marriage contract), s. 54 (separation agreement), s. 55 (formalities), s. 56 (setting aside)

tested_concepts: FLA s.52 vs s.54 distinct instruments not interchangeable
separation agreement correct for post-separation settlement
wrong contract type creates ambiguity and risk
FLA ss.55-56 apply by instrument type
marriage contract not automatically invalid post-separation

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Custody/Parenting Provisions in Domestic Contracts — Best Interests Override FLA s.56(1)
fact_pattern: A separation agreement signed three years ago specifies that the children will live primarily with the father and the mother will have alternate weekend parenting time. At the time, both parents agreed this was best. The children have since expressed a strong desire to live primarily with the mother, and the mother now applies to court for a change in primary parenting based on the children's changed needs. The father argues the separation agreement is binding on the court.
call_of_question: Is the parenting provision in the separation agreement binding on the court in a subsequent parenting application?

options.A: Yes — separation agreements on parenting matters are fully binding on the court and may not be overridden.
options.B: No — under FLA s.56(1), the court shall disregard a domestic contract provision respecting education, moral training, custody, or access to a child where, in the opinion of the court, doing so is in the best interests of the child; the agreement is a relevant starting point but not binding.
options.C: No — parenting agreements are void for public policy reasons because only courts may determine parenting matters.
options.D: Yes — parenting provisions in a separation agreement are binding unless the child has reached 12 years of age and objects.

explanation: Family Law Act s.56(1) expressly provides that in the determination of matters respecting education, moral training, or custody of or access to a child, the court is not bound by a provision of a domestic contract and shall disregard it if, in the opinion of the court, to do so is in the best interests of the child. The separation agreement is a relevant factor the court will consider and give weight to — particularly if it was negotiated by informed parties who had the children's interests in mind — but it is not binding. The court must independently assess the current best interests of the children, including their changed preferences and needs. This is a statutory protection ensuring the paramountcy of the best interests principle.
why_A_wrong: FLA s.56(1) expressly provides that the court is not bound by domestic contract provisions on custody and access and shall disregard them where the best interests of the child require it. The contract is a consideration, not a determinative constraint.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. FLA s.56(1) makes parenting provisions in domestic contracts non-binding on the court; the best interests of the child always take precedence over the contractual arrangement.
why_C_wrong: Parenting agreements are not void for public policy. They are valid contracts, enforceable between the parties as contracts, but the court is not bound by them and may override them in the child's best interests under FLA s.56(1).
why_D_wrong: There is no age-12 threshold at which parenting provisions become binding in Ontario. FLA s.56(1) applies regardless of the age of the child. The best interests analysis applies to children of all ages.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Parenting Provisions — FLA s.56(1) Court Not Bound
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 56(1)

tested_concepts: FLA s.56(1) court not bound by parenting provisions

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Cohabitation Agreement Converting to Marriage Contract on Marriage
fact_pattern: Julia and Michael entered into a cohabitation agreement two years ago before moving in together. The agreement addressed property division and support in the event their relationship ended. They have since decided to marry. Julia's lawyer advises her that the cohabitation agreement automatically becomes void on marriage and must be renegotiated. Michael's lawyer disagrees.
call_of_question: Under the Family Law Act, what is the effect of marriage on a pre-existing cohabitation agreement?

options.A: A cohabitation agreement is automatically void upon marriage; the parties must enter into a new marriage contract to have any contractual protections.
options.B: Under FLA s.53, a cohabitation agreement entered into by persons who subsequently marry does not become void on the marriage; it is deemed to be a marriage contract.
options.C: A cohabitation agreement survives marriage unchanged but loses its contractual force; it becomes only advisory.
options.D: A cohabitation agreement converts to a separation agreement upon marriage, applicable only if they subsequently separate.

explanation: Family Law Act s.53(2) provides that if persons who have entered into a cohabitation agreement marry each other, the agreement is deemed to be a marriage contract. This automatic conversion means that upon marriage, the cohabitation agreement takes on the legal status and effect of a marriage contract, without the need for renegotiation or a new document. Julia's lawyer's advice that the agreement becomes void is incorrect. The parties should review the terms to confirm they remain appropriate and obtain legal advice, but the existing agreement is legally preserved by s.53(2).
why_A_wrong: FLA s.53(2) expressly preserves a cohabitation agreement upon marriage by converting it to a marriage contract by operation of law. The agreement is not void; it is upgraded in status.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. FLA s.53(2) deems a cohabitation agreement to be a marriage contract upon the parties marrying, preserving the agreement without requiring renegotiation.
why_C_wrong: A cohabitation agreement deemed to be a marriage contract under FLA s.53(2) retains its full contractual force. It does not become merely advisory; it is a legally binding marriage contract.
why_D_wrong: A cohabitation agreement does not convert to a separation agreement upon marriage. Under FLA s.53(2), it becomes a marriage contract — an entirely different legal instrument that governs rights during the marriage and upon its dissolution.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Cohabitation Agreement Converting to Marriage Contract — FLA s.53(2)
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 53(2)

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Limitation Period for Setting Aside a Domestic Contract
fact_pattern: Helen signed a marriage contract 12 years ago, before her marriage. She now claims that her husband failed to disclose significant assets before signing. She has known about the non-disclosure for approximately 5 years but only now seeks to set aside the contract. Her husband argues the claim to set aside the contract is statute-barred.
call_of_question: Is Helen's application to set aside the marriage contract under FLA s.56(4)(a) subject to a limitation period, and if so, has it expired?

options.A: There is no limitation period for setting aside a domestic contract; such applications may be brought at any time.
options.B: The application to set aside a domestic contract under FLA s.56(4)(a) is subject to the general two-year limitation period under the Limitations Act, 2002, running from the date of discovery of the non-disclosure; if Helen has known of the non-disclosure for approximately 5 years, her claim may be statute-barred.
options.C: The limitation period for setting aside a domestic contract is six years from the date the contract was signed.
options.D: The limitation period is 10 years from the date of the marriage.

explanation: Applications to set aside domestic contracts under FLA s.56(4) are civil law claims subject to the Limitations Act, 2002. The basic limitation period is two years from the date the claim was discovered (s.4 of the Limitations Act, 2002). The discovery rule means the period runs from the date Helen knew or ought to have known of the non-disclosure and that it would be an appropriate basis for seeking relief. If Helen has known of the non-disclosure for approximately 5 years, the two-year limitation may have expired approximately 3 years ago, potentially making her claim statute-barred. The husband's argument has merit.
why_A_wrong: Applications to set aside domestic contracts are civil claims subject to the Limitations Act, 2002 two-year limitation period. There is no unlimited time to bring such applications.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Setting aside a domestic contract is subject to the Limitations Act, 2002 two-year period from discovery; knowing of non-disclosure for 5 years likely means the claim is statute-barred.
why_C_wrong: No six-year limitation period applies to setting aside domestic contracts. The Limitations Act, 2002 basic period is two years from discovery, not six years from signing.
why_D_wrong: There is no 10-year limitation period tied to the date of marriage for domestic contract challenges. The Limitations Act, 2002 discovery-based two-year period governs.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Limitation Period — Setting Aside Domestic Contract FLA s.56(4)
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 56(4); Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B, s. 4

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Disclosure Obligation Before Signing — FLA s.56(4)(a) and Miglin Analysis
fact_pattern: Before signing a separation agreement, both parties exchanged financial statements. William disclosed his home and bank accounts but did not disclose a significant outstanding debt — a personal guarantee for $300,000 on a business loan — despite being asked about liabilities. His spouse Sandra signed the agreement not knowing about the guarantee. The equalization payment in the agreement heavily favoured William. Sandra later discovers the undisclosed guarantee and seeks to set aside the agreement.
call_of_question: Under FLA s.56(4)(a), what is the legal significance of William's failure to disclose the $300,000 guarantee, and how does it affect the separation agreement?

options.A: Non-disclosure of debts is not a ground for setting aside under FLA s.56(4)(a); only non-disclosure of assets is covered.
options.B: Under FLA s.56(4)(a), a court may set aside a domestic contract where a party failed to disclose significant assets or significant debts or other liabilities existing when the domestic contract was made; a $300,000 personal guarantee is a significant liability, and its non-disclosure despite a direct inquiry constitutes a strong ground for setting aside the agreement.
options.C: Non-disclosure of a contingent liability (a guarantee) is not sufficient to set aside a separation agreement because the guarantee may never be called.
options.D: The agreement cannot be set aside because Sandra had the opportunity to conduct independent inquiry and obtain her own financial information.

explanation: Family Law Act s.56(4)(a) provides that a court may set aside a domestic contract or a provision where a party 'failed to disclose to the other significant assets or significant debts or other liabilities existing when the domestic contract was made.' The provision expressly covers both assets and debts/liabilities — not only assets. A personal guarantee of $300,000 is a 'significant liability' within the meaning of s.56(4)(a), particularly where it was not disclosed despite a direct inquiry about liabilities. The non-disclosure of this significant liability is a clear statutory ground for seeking to set aside the agreement. The court has discretion and will consider all circumstances, including the impact on the equalization result had the guarantee been disclosed.
why_A_wrong: FLA s.56(4)(a) expressly includes 'significant debts or other liabilities' as well as significant assets. Non-disclosure of a significant debt or contingent liability is an equally valid ground for setting aside.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. FLA s.56(4)(a) covers non-disclosure of both assets and debts/liabilities; a $300,000 undisclosed guarantee despite a direct inquiry is a significant liability that may ground a successful application to set aside.
why_C_wrong: The contingent nature of a guarantee does not necessarily remove it from the scope of significant liabilities under FLA s.56(4)(a). Courts assess the significance of the undisclosed liability in the context of the agreement and the parties' overall financial positions.
why_D_wrong: The obligation of disclosure rests on each party to the agreement, not solely on the other party to investigate and discover independently. FLA s.56(4)(a) does not require Sandra to have done independent investigation; William's active non-disclosure despite a direct inquiry remains a ground for setting aside.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Setting Aside — Non-Disclosure of Significant Debts FLA s.56(4)(a)
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 56(4)(a)

tested_concepts: FLA s.56(4)(a) covers debts not only assets
